source.module: Introduction to Matplotlib and Basic Plotting

topicTags: Introduction to Matplotlib and Basic Plotting

content.problemStatement:
Bar charts are used to represent categorical data with rectangular bars. \
The height of each bar represents the value of the category. Some applications of bar charts are as follows:
- Comparison is Key: Bar charts excel at comparing values across different categories. This makes them ideal for business, social sciences, and many other fields where comparing group performance or characteristics is important.
- Categorical Data: They're best suited for categorical data (like products, regions, demographics) or discrete numerical data that can be grouped into categories.
- Survey Analysis: Bar charts are a go-to choice for visualizing survey results, especially for multiple choice or Likert scale questions.

Check the code given in the IDE - new points to note here are as follows
- Data generation - We have directly created lists instead of creating NumPy arrays
- Plot generation
  - plt.bar(categories, values, color=colors) - This creates the bar chart
     - categories are used for the x-axis positions of the bars
     - values determine the height of each bar
     - color=colors assigns each bar a color from the colors list
  - plt.grid(True, axis='y') - The axis='y' parameter ensures that only horizontal lines are drawn.

### Task
Update the code given in the IDE to get the following output.
- You can assume any population as per your choice. 
- You need to enable only vertical lines.
- You need to generate a horizontal bar chart using plt.barh(countries, population) - here countries becomes the y axis and population is the x axis.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
